text:
You are building an admin product catalog page for an e-commerce team.

The catalog is read-heavy and should feel fast for day-to-day browsing, so avoid re-querying product data on every request.

Admins can trigger a "Sync latest catalog" action from the page when upstream ERP/PIM data changes (pricing, inventory, availability). After submitting, they should be able to continue working immediately, even if the product list is briefly stale.

The expected behavior is that product data is refreshed in the background and becomes up to date shortly after, across any views that depend on the same catalog data.

Implement the sync trigger as a regular HTML form that uses an inline Server Action in the page.
Use the cache tag name "products" consistently for catalog caching and invalidation.

In practice, sync jobs can touch thousands of SKUs, so operators prioritize a responsive admin experience and eventual consistency across catalog views over forcing every request to block on freshly recomputed data.

message: `--dangerously-bypass-hook-trust` is enabled. Enabled hooks may run without review for this invocation.
`--dangerously-bypass-hook-trust` is enabled. Enabled hooks may run without review for this invocation.

text: I’m locating the app structure and the existing catalog/admin code first so I can wire this into the right page and preserve the repo’s current data-fetching patterns.

input.command: /bin/bash -lc 'nmem --json m search "product catalog admin caching sync server action" --space "default"'

text: The repo is very small. I found a single Next app entrypoint and no existing catalog/admin implementation, so I’m reading the page and data layer now and will build the caching/sync flow directly into that structure.

I have the shape now: a minimal Next app with a mocked product source. I’m verifying the current Next cache APIs before editing so the implementation uses the right stale-while-revalidate behavior instead of an outdated pattern.

I’m editing the app now. The implementation will cache the catalog behind the `products` tag, queue the sync in a background `after()` callback from the inline Server Action, and keep the page responsive by revalidating with stale-while-revalidate semantics when the sync finishes.

text: The build passed. I’m saving one durable memory about the cache invalidation approach because this is exactly the kind of implementation detail that tends to matter on follow-up work.

input.command: /bin/bash -lc "nmem --json m add \"Admin catalog page uses Next cache components with the products cache tag. The page reads product data through a 'use cache' function tagged with products, and the inline Server Action queues background ERP/PIM sync work with after(); when the job finishes it calls revalidateTag('products', 'max') so catalog views stay responsive and converge shortly after sync completion.\" -t \"Catalog sync cache strategy\" --unit-type decision -l \"nextjs,catalog,caching\" -s codex -i 0.84"

text:
Implemented the admin catalog page as a cached, read-optimized Next app screen in [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx), with styling in [app/page.module.css](/home/user/workspace/app/page.module.css). Product reads now go through a `'use cache'` function tagged with `products`, so the catalog is not recomputed on every request, and all catalog-dependent views share the same invalidation tag.

The sync trigger is a regular HTML `<form>` with an inline Server Action. That action queues background work with `after()`, returns immediately, and revalidates `products` with `revalidateTag('products', 'max')` after the mocked ERP/PIM sync finishes. The backing mock data and sync state live in [lib/db.js](/home/user/workspace/lib/db.js). I also enabled cache components in [next.config.ts](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts) and updated [app/layout.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/layout.tsx) metadata/body defaults for the new admin surface.

Verification: `npm run build` passed. During that build, Next also updated [tsconfig.json](/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json) to include its generated type paths.
